title: Imam bayildi
subtitle: Whole aubergines stuffed with onion, tomato, and garlic, baked slowly in olive oil
excerpt: Halved aubergines filled with a slow-cooked mixture of onion, garlic, and tomato, then baked in a very generous quantity of olive oil until the flesh has completely softened and absorbed the oil. A Turkish classic that is served at room temperature, never hot. The name means the imam fainted, supposedly at the quantity of olive oil.
sourceNotes: Imam bayildi is documented in Turkish cookery literature from the Ottoman period. The dish is mentioned in nineteenth-century Turkish household cookery accounts and is described in several twentieth-century surveys of Turkish cuisine. The olive oil quantity is the defining characteristic of the dish and should not be reduced significantly.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make up to 2 days ahead. The dish is best served at room temperature and improves after a few hours in the fridge.

content.text: Imam bayildi uses an unusual quantity of olive oil — more than feels right, and the point of the dish. The aubergines are baked in it, slowly absorbing the oil over an hour until the flesh is completely soft, silky, and saturated. Attempting to reduce the oil to make the dish lighter defeats the purpose. The result is not greasy if you use a good olive oil and bake it gently; it is rich, sweet, and deeply savoury.

The filling must be cooked well before going into the aubergine — soft, sweet onions with collapsed tomato and garlic. It is not a raw vegetable mixture. The dish is served at room temperature or slightly cool, never hot. It improves significantly after a few hours.

Score the cut surface of each {{aubergine}} aubergine half in a cross-hatch pattern without cutting through the skin. Sprinkle generously with salt and leave cut-side down on a rack for 20 minutes to draw out moisture. Pat dry with kitchen paper. Heat the oven to 180°C (fan 160°C).

Heat 40 ml of the {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} of olive oil in a wide frying pan over medium heat. Add the {{onion}} onions and cook for 15 minutes until very soft and starting to colour. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic and cook for 3 more minutes. Add the {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tinned tomatoes and {{caster-sugar}} of sugar. Cook for 10 minutes until the tomatoes have reduced and the mixture is thick and concentrated. Season with the remaining {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt.

Arrange the aubergine halves cut-side up in a baking dish. Spoon the tomato and onion filling into each cavity, pressing it into the cuts. Pour the remaining 80 ml of olive oil over and around the aubergines. Add 100 ml of water to the dish. Cover tightly with foil and bake for 40 minutes. Remove the foil and bake for a further 20 minutes until the aubergines are completely soft and the oil is absorbed. Leave to cool to room temperature. Scatter over {{parsley-flat}} of parsley and squeeze over the juice from the {{lemon}} lemon.

items.symptom: Aubergines are still firm after baking
items.cause: Not enough time or oven too low
items.fix: Cover and return to the oven for 20 more minutes; the flesh must be completely collapsed and very soft

items.symptom: Dish is oily rather than silky
items.cause: Oven temperature too high, causing the oil to fry rather than absorb
items.fix: Keep the temperature at 180°C maximum; the oil should bubble gently, not sizzle; adding a little water to the dish helps

items.cause: Tomatoes not reduced enough before stuffing
items.fix: Cook the tomato filling until it is properly thick and concentrated — it should hold its shape on a spoon, not run

content.text: Imam bayildi is one of the most famous dishes of the Ottoman kitchen — and the name is part of its fame. The story goes that the imam (Islamic prayer leader) fainted when he tasted it: either from pleasure, or from shock at the quantity of expensive olive oil his wife had used to make it. The dish spread from the Ottoman court into Greek, Turkish, and wider Mediterranean cooking, and versions of it appear across the region. In Greece, the equivalent dish is called papoutsakia (little shoes). The olive oil is not negotiable.
